Great work on reproing and coming up with a potential fix! However, I don't think this is the right one. The reason for pre-decoding images in a batch before training, is so that we don't have to do it every time we read an image. We could theoretically also do it using memoization, which might reduce initial latency. Each image gets read once per epoch, so if we re-decode the images every epoch, then we are back to decoding every image every time. The only difference now is that we do all of them in a batch, which I don't see the point of, since that will leave likely leave the GPU idle longer.

I have been discussing with @nickjong that perhaps after the performance improvements he made, we no longer need to pre-decode images. I think we should benchmark this, since then the fix is simply to remove this pre-processed column altogether.

I also want to point out that this only reduces the size of each image. If you make your images so large that their encoded size is the size of the decoded one in the repro, then presumably this issue would re-appear, right?

Added a FAQ at the bottom with this answer, thought it worth doing.

## `start` and `end`

I think that for AnnoSpans, `start` and `end` are sort of… part of the core definition. But for a SpanGroup, they have no intrinsic meaning — they're just labels — and I think it makes sense to reference them to the lowest start and highest end of the base_spans. Otherwise, if you, say, applied the function that's defined elsewhere here which trims base spans to one of the base spans, and it was part of a SpanGroup, it would be wrong.

## Creating new SpanGroups vs. modifying an existing SpanGroup

And the reason for that is that, if there was code elsewhere that was using that name to access the object, changing the object in place could cause unexpected behavior, and it's better to create new objects?

I still think that this is useful, but I'm thinking about a future use case where, for instance, we could use this sort of class to generate incidents. (I guess this is what's happening in the structured data annotator).

It seems to me that creating new SpanGroups each time you want to add a property to a SpanGroup would be fine, as long as you had a method for instantiating a SpanGroup by copying and modifying an existing SpanGroup? I don't think I'm making my argument clearly here.

Here's my question, in an example. Say you wanted to add a date to an infection span based on a set of rules. You identify the spans you want to combine, and do `group1 = SpanGroup([infectionspan, datespan])`.

Then you have code that identifies a GeoSpan that's nearby. If you do `group2 = SpanGroup([group1, geospan])`, then you have a weird nested structure, and so on, if you got to the point where you were resolving multiple properties in that way. You're probably talking about doing something like `group2 = SpanGroup([group1.base_spans, geospan])`, which works better.

Something like `group2 = copy(group1).add_span(geospan)` would both copy the object and add a span (this is imagining an `.add_span()` method which appended that AnnoSpan to the `base_span` list), but this again seems like a rather unsatisfying solution.

This implementation isn't ideal. The existing order of operations is:

1. Apply all Function Event configuration
1. Apply API configuration

This makes it hard to apply a DefaultAuthorizer, especially when 'NONE' is defined on the Function Event. Current logic is to add a 'NONE' entry to the Swagger and then remove it when DefaultAuthorizer comes through. Similarly, DefaultAuthorizer won't be added if it detects an existing entry in `security` which matches a key defined in `Api.Auth.Authorizers`.

Ideally we would add DefaultAuthorizer first and then remove/override as needed, but I'd rather not complicate the order of operations (unless there's already precedent of that which I'm not aware of).

The test cases you changed represent a use case that is not "fine without it". Those test cases are supported by a reasonable interpretation of the existing documentation.

> I haven't seen a better suggestions to [report] multiple different errors for different (nested) fields from whole-schema validator

You could break your whole-schema validator up into several schema validators, so that you can raise one `ValidationError` per error message. The multiprocessing module makes these proxy objects automatically for us.  I think it uses introspection to create something with all the same methods, but which pickles the args and kwargs to these methods and sends them through the multprocessing.Pipe back to the original object, which then calls the method.  Then the return value is again pickled and sent back through the pipe to the process that called it.

It's possible there is a more elegant way to check if we have a proxy object.  I'm certainly not a python multiprocessing expert, but I couldn't come up with anything more straightforward than this.  I suppose you could try to access an attribute and catch the exception that a proxy would raise, since it doesn't copy over all the attributes, just the public methods.  But that seems even more convoluted than this isinstance check.

hey! So, we used to have code like this (simplified):

```py
class Selector:
    def extract(self):
        # ...
    get = extract

class SelectorList:
    def extract(self):
        return [s.extract() for s in self]
    getall = extract
```

In https://github.com/scrapy/parsel/pull/113/commits/5739a929fe7d124f8faafea8a4ffd4ff4e95f20d it is changed to this:

```py
class Selector:
    def get(self):
        # ...
    extract = get

class SelectorList:
    def getall(self):
        return [s.get() for s in self]
    extract = getall
```

First, there is an issue with aliases: if one overrides e.g. Selector.extract in a subclass, then Selector.get is not changed automatically, alias needs to be re-added in a subclass. It is not necessarily a problem, and it saves us a function call; also, nothing changed regarding this in the "blessing" PRs.

If we make one of these methods call another, instead of having aliases, then overriding would work as expected - but only for one of the methods, and we're to choose for which method. I'm not sure it is a big win.

The backwards incompatible change here is a change in implementation of methods: getall used to call .extract, while now it calls .get; there are several other changes like this. It means that Selector.extract override in a subclass no longer has an effect on SelectorList, while .get override starts to have an effect.

I made this change while fixing a docstring for .getall. It said that getall calls .extract methods of Selector, not .get, which I was thinking is not good. 

So, an exact backwards incompatibility is that in custom Selector subclasses .get should be overridden instead of .extract to take an effect in `SelectorList.getall`, `SelectorList.extract`, `SelecrorList.getall`, `SelectorList.extract_first`, `Selector.__repr__`, `Selector.re`, `Selector.bool`. It is not a problem as soon as Selector subclass sets up aliases properly, i.e. it is only an issue when a subclass is overriding .extract without fixing .get alias.

`PASS` should only be returned when it wants the TrialRunner to continue executing without fetching a new trial. 

If it does NOT want the TrialRunner to continue executing, it can block until the algorithm finishes calculating whatever it needs to calculate. 

There are a few cases in mind:
1. When the algorithm is throttled by maximum concurrency
2. When the algorithm is throttled by some internal calculation. In this case, you'll either need to block per calculation or you want to let the calculation run on a separate thread.

If the SearchAlgorithm wants to flip between blocking and passing, then it could do that - after all, it knows the trials that it generates and it also knows the trials that complete/error/get killed.

DRY is not only about lines of code. Consider a case where there is an issue with the flow in line 168, and the developer decides to change it from cudaDeviceSynchronize to another API. Without DRY, the developer will need to remember to look for other places in the code that call the API. Sometimes, this will be missed. With DRY, different parts of the code are funneled to the one function or whatever that has this logic, and by updating in one location all code paths are updated. Makes sense?
